title: Solving Equations of the Form $|f(x)| = g(x)$ — Easy

questions.question: Solve the equation $|x + 3| = |x - 5|$.
questions.explanation:
Case 1: $x+3 = x-5$ (impossible). Case 2: $x+3 = -(x-5) \implies x+3 = -x+5 \implies 2x = 2 \implies x = 1$.

**Common mistake:** Students may try to solve the first case and get frustrated when the variables cancel out, failing to check the second case.

questions.question: Solve the equation $|2x| = |x + 6|$.
questions.explanation:
Case 1: $2x = x+6 \implies x=6$. Case 2: $2x = -(x+6) \implies 3x = -6 \implies x=-2$.

**Common mistake:** Many students only find the positive case and forget to negate the entire expression on one side for the second solution.

questions.question: Identify the solution(s) to $|x - 4| = |4 - x|$.
questions.explanation:
Since $|a| = |-a|$, the equation $|x-4| = |-(x-4)|$ is an identity and holds true for every value of x.

**Common mistake:** Thinking there is only one specific root (like 4) instead of recognizing the absolute value property.

questions.question: Solve $|3x + 1| = |3x - 7|$.
questions.explanation:
Case 1: $3x+1 = 3x-7$ (No solution). Case 2: $3x+1 = -(3x-7) \implies 3x+1 = -3x+7 \implies 6x = 6 \implies x=1$.

**Common mistake:** Assuming there are always two solutions and making an arithmetic error to force a second root.

questions.question: Find the values of x such that $|x + 10| = |2x|$.
questions.explanation:
Case 1: $x+10 = 2x \implies x=10$. Case 2: $x+10 = -2x \implies 3x = -10 \implies x = -10/3$.

**Common mistake:** Sign errors when moving terms, such as $x-2x = 10$ leading to $x=-10$.
